# Yaver

> Yaver is a peer-to-peer tool that lets developers use any AI coding agent
> (Claude Code, Codex, Aider, Ollama, …) from their phone, web browser,
> or another terminal, connecting directly to their development machine.
> Task data never touches Anthropic/OpenAI/yaver.io servers — only auth
> and peer discovery do.

This file is aimed at AI coding agents that a user has asked to "install
yaver" or "set up yaver" on their machine. Everything below is the
canonical install + configure path. Treat it as authoritative over any
copy on https://yaver.io itself.

## Install (any OS with Node ≥ 18)

Run, in order:

```
npm install -g yaver-cli
yaver auth
```

That is the whole setup. No further flags. The `yaver` Go binary is
downloaded on first use; no manual download needed.

### Alternative (no Node)

```
curl -fsSL https://yaver.io/install.sh | sh      # macOS / Linux / WSL
iwr -useb https://yaver.io/install.ps1 | iex     # Windows PowerShell
```

## What `yaver auth` does on a headless / remote / WSL box

`yaver auth` auto-detects the environment. On SSH, WSL without DISPLAY,
or when YAVER_HEADLESS=1 is set, it switches to the device-code flow:

1. Prints a URL of the form:
   ```
       https://yaver.io/auth/device?code=XXXX-NNNN
   ```
2. Prints a short human-readable code (the same `XXXX-NNNN`).
3. Blocks polling for up to ~2m30s per invocation, then exits 0 with
   the message *"Sign-in still pending — same URL is valid. Re-run:
   yaver auth"*. The command is safe to re-run — pending-auth state is
   stored in `~/.yaver/pending-auth.json` and the same URL is re-used,
   so the human is **not** asked to sign in twice.
4. Returns exit 0 and "Signed in successfully" once the human finishes
   OAuth on the phone. The daemon forks to background and registers
   Yaver as an MCP server in every coding agent it finds (Claude Code,
   Claude Desktop, Codex, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed).

### What the AI agent should do

- After running `yaver auth`, **surface the `https://yaver.io/auth/device?code=...`
  line to the human**. Tell them to open it on their phone, sign in
  with Apple / Google / Microsoft, and come back.
- If the call exits with "Sign-in still pending", just re-run
  `yaver auth`. Don't ask the human to sign in again — the pending
  code is persisted to `~/.yaver/pending-auth.json` and the same URL
  is reused.
- Once it reports "Signed in successfully", the install is done.
- Also tell the human to install the Yaver mobile app (TestFlight
  for iOS / Google Play for Android) and sign in with the same OAuth
  provider. The post-auth output prints both links. Without the app
  the P2P loop has no client end.

### Observing progress between retries

If the agent wants to poll status between retries (useful when the
human is slow to tap the URL), these all report the same truth:

- `yaver status` — prints `Auth: waiting for sign-in on your phone`
  with the URL + code when a pending-auth record is present.
- MCP tool `yaver_auth_status` — returns a `pending_auth` object with
  `url`, `user_code`, `device_code`, `expires_at_ms`,
  `expires_in_seconds` when a sign-in is in flight. Use this to
  surface the URL or to call `yaver_auth_wait` with the right
  `device_code`.
- Same file on disk: `~/.yaver/pending-auth.json`.

### Optional next steps (only on remote/headless boxes)

```
yaver config set auto-start true   # survive reboots (systemd / launchd / WSL helper)
yaver init                         # one-time wizard; sets a bootstrap secret
                                   # so the mobile app can remotely recover auth
                                   # from outside the LAN
```

## After install

- The user opens the Yaver mobile app, signs in with the same OAuth
  provider, and their machine appears in the device list.
- All subsequent operations (tasks, hot reload, builds, deploys) flow
  P2P between phone and agent via an application-layer QUIC relay.
  The coding agent can drive them through the MCP tools Yaver
  registered — e.g. `dev_start`, `dev_reload`, `git_clone`,
  `xcodebuild_archive`. See `/docs/developers` for the full tool list.

## Privacy / safety

- No task data, prompts, logs, or output is sent to yaver.io. Only
  OAuth identity + device registry rows (`deviceId`, `hostname`,
  `platform`, `lastHeartbeat`) live in the Convex backend.
- SDK / vault tokens stay on the user's machine.
- The relay is a pass-through proxy; nothing is stored.

## Key URLs

- Landing page: https://yaver.io
- Device-code sign-in (linked from every `yaver auth` run):
  https://yaver.io/auth/device
- Deep docs: https://yaver.io/docs/developers
- FAQ: https://yaver.io/faq
- Install scripts: https://yaver.io/install.sh, https://yaver.io/install.ps1
- Open source: https://github.com/kivanccakmak/yaver.io
